Q1.bottleneck: Hash aggregation overhead for tiny group count (4 groups)
Q1.root_cause: std::unordered_map creates heap allocations, hash collisions, and cache misses for only 4 groups (returnflag × linestatus). Perfect hash aggregation with direct array indexing would eliminate all hashing overhead. Currently spending ~500-700ms on hash table operations that should take <10ms.
Q1.benchmark_gap: 6.1x slower than DuckDB (199ms). DuckDB likely uses perfect hashing + vectorized aggregation. Expected improvement: 3-5x with perfect hashing alone.

Q3.bottleneck: Multiple hash table probes per lineitem row + full result materialization before LIMIT 10
Q3.root_cause: For each of 32M qualifying lineitem rows (54% selectivity on l_shipdate filter), we: (1) hash l_orderkey, (2) probe qualifying_orders hash table, (3) hash composite group key, (4) probe/update aggregates hash table. That's 64M hash operations + 32M hash table probes. Then we sort ALL aggregates before taking top 10. With ~300K aggregate groups, we're sorting 300K rows to get 10. Should use min-heap of size 10 during aggregation phase.
Q3.benchmark_gap: 16.1x slower than DuckDB (135ms). Largest gap of all queries. DuckDB likely uses: (1) bloom filter to eliminate 80% of lineitem rows before join, (2) top-K optimization avoiding full sort, (3) possibly sorted/indexed join on orderkey. Expected improvement: 8-12x with bloom filter + top-K heap.

Q6.bottleneck: Sequential scan of all 60M lineitem rows without block skipping
Q6.root_cause: Lineitem is not sorted by l_shipdate, so we cannot use zone maps or binary search. We scan all 60M rows, applying date filter (14% selective = keep 8.4M rows). Without zone maps, we touch all data. With zone maps on sorted l_shipdate, we'd skip ~86% of 64KB blocks without reading them. Additional issue: scalar filter evaluation (no SIMD), tuple-at-a-time processing (no vectorization).
Q6.benchmark_gap: 9.5x slower than DuckDB (27ms). DuckDB likely has sorted storage + zone maps (skip blocks) + vectorized execution. Expected improvement: 5-8x with zone maps, another 2x with SIMD filters.

analysis.overall: GenDB iteration 1 implements basic columnar storage with hash-based joins and aggregations. Performance is 6-16x slower than DuckDB across all queries. Primary bottlenecks: (1) Q3's multiple hash table probes per row + full materialization before LIMIT, (2) lack of zone maps for date filtering (all queries filter on dates), (3) inefficient aggregation strategies (hash table for 4 groups in Q1, full sort for top-10 in Q3), (4) no SIMD or vectorization. The 141-second data load time suggests string parsing overhead. Total query execution time (3.6s) is dominated by Q3 (60% of time). Optimization priority: Q3 > Q6 > Q1, focusing on bloom filters, top-K heaps, zone maps, and perfect hashing.

recommendations.description: Replace the current two-phase approach (full hash aggregation → sort all groups → take 10) with incremental top-K tracking. Maintain a min-heap of size 10 during the aggregation phase. For each (l_orderkey, revenue) tuple, if revenue > heap.min(), evict minimum and insert new tuple. This avoids: (1) storing 300K aggregate entries (saves ~15MB), (2) sorting 300K rows (saves ~100ms). Implementation: use std::priority_queue<ResultRow, std::vector<ResultRow>, CompareRevenue> with max size 10. In queries/q3.cpp, replace std::unordered_map<Q3GroupKey, Q3Aggregate> with direct top-K heap insertion after computing revenue. Expected improvement: 200-400ms (avoid sorting 300K groups).
recommendations.expected_improvement: 200-400ms reduction (9-18% of Q3 time). Low-hanging fruit with minimal code change. Reduces Q3 from 2178ms to ~1800-2000ms.
recommendations.knowledge_source: query-execution/top-k-optimization (implicit in aggregation strategies) + novel application

recommendations.technique: Bloom filter on qualifying_orders to skip lineitem rows early
recommendations.description: After building qualifying_orders hash table (contains ~7M orderkeys after customer join + date filter), build a bloom filter (512KB for 1% false positive rate at 7M keys). During lineitem scan, probe bloom filter BEFORE computing revenue or probing hash table. This eliminates ~53M out of 60M lineitem rows (88% rejection rate: only 12% of lineitems match the 7M qualifying orders). Bloom filter probe is 5-10 CPU cycles vs 50-100 cycles for hash table probe. Implementation: Add BloomFilter class (bit array + k=3 hash functions using Murmur3). In queries/q3.cpp after building qualifying_orders, iterate and insert all orderkeys into bloom filter. In lineitem scan loop, add 'if (!bloom_filter.contains(lineitem.l_orderkey[i])) continue;' before hash table probe. Expected improvement: 800-1200ms (avoid 53M hash table probes).
recommendations.expected_improvement: 800-1200ms reduction (37-55% of Q3 time). Bloom filter eliminates vast majority of hash probes. Reduces Q3 from ~2000ms to ~800-1200ms after top-K optimization.

recommendations.technique: Perfect hash aggregation with direct array indexing
recommendations.description: Q1 groups by (l_returnflag, l_linestatus), which produces only 4 groups: (A,F), (N,F), (N,O), (R,F). Current std::unordered_map has ~100-200 cycle overhead per update (hash computation, bucket lookup, equality check). Replace with direct array indexing: create array Q1Aggregate agg_table[9] (3 returnflag values × 3 linestatus values = max 9 combinations). Map (returnflag, linestatus) to index: int idx = (returnflag - 'A') * 3 + (linestatus - 'F'). Direct array access is 1 cycle vs 100+ for hash table. Implementation: In queries/q1.cpp, replace std::unordered_map<Q1GroupKey, Q1Aggregate> with std::array<Q1Aggregate, 9> agg_table, initialize all to zero. Use direct indexing: agg_table[idx].sum_qty += quantity. At end, iterate array and output only non-zero entries. Expected improvement: 400-600ms (eliminate hash table overhead).
recommendations.expected_improvement: 400-600ms reduction (33-50% of Q1 time). Perfect hashing is optimal for tiny group counts. Reduces Q1 from 1210ms to ~600-800ms.

recommendations.description: Q6 applies 4 predicates to 60M rows: (1) l_shipdate >= start_date, (2) l_shipdate < end_date, (3) l_discount >= 0.05, (4) l_discount <= 0.07, (5) l_quantity < 24. Current scalar code processes 1 row per cycle. AVX2 can process 8 int32 comparisons or 4 double comparisons per instruction. Vectorize date filtering: load 8 shipdates, compare against bounds, create bitmask. Only process rows that pass date filter with discount/quantity checks. Implementation: In queries/q6.cpp, add AVX2 intrinsics. Process lineitem in chunks of 8: load l_shipdate[i:i+8] into __m256i, compare with _mm256_cmpgt_epi32, get mask with _mm256_movemask_epi8. For passing rows, load l_discount/l_quantity and apply scalar checks. Expected improvement: 100-150ms (2-3x speedup on filter evaluation). Note: Most time is still memory bandwidth, so improvement is modest.
recommendations.expected_improvement: 100-150ms reduction (39-58% of Q6 time). SIMD helps but memory bandwidth is the true bottleneck. Reduces Q6 from 257ms to ~100-150ms.

recommendations.technique: Sort lineitem table by l_shipdate during data load
recommendations.description: All 3 queries filter on l_shipdate with high selectivity (Q1: 98%, Q3: 54%, Q6: 14%). Sorting lineitem by l_shipdate enables: (1) Binary search to find date range boundaries, (2) Zone map effectiveness (tight min/max per block), (3) Early termination for range queries, (4) Sequential access after filter (better prefetching). Current random date distribution means zone maps would have wide ranges (min=1992, max=1998 in every block) → no pruning. Sorted data creates tight zones (min=1994-01-01, max=1994-01-10 in block 1000). Implementation: In storage/storage.cpp after loading lineitem, add: std::vector<size_t> indices(lineitem.size()); std::iota(indices.begin(), indices.end(), 0); std::sort(indices.begin(), indices.end(), [&](size_t a, size_t b) { return lineitem.l_shipdate[a] < lineitem.l_shipdate[b]; }); Then reorder all columns using indices. Expected improvement: Enables zone maps (next priority), ~10-20s overhead during load (one-time cost).
recommendations.expected_improvement: No direct query speedup (enables zone maps). Adds 10-20s to data load time (trade-off: longer load for faster queries). This is a prerequisite for zone map optimization.

recommendations.technique: Zone maps on sorted l_shipdate for block skipping
recommendations.description: After sorting lineitem by l_shipdate (priority 5), build zone maps: for every 8K rows, store (min_shipdate, max_shipdate). Q6 filters for 1994-01-01 to 1995-01-01 (14% selectivity). With random data, all blocks have min=1992, max=1998 → skip 0%. With sorted data, only blocks with [min,max] overlapping [1994-01-01, 1995-01-01] need scanning → skip ~86% of blocks. Implementation: Add struct ZoneMap { int32_t min_date; int32_t max_date; size_t start_row; size_t end_row; }; std::vector<ZoneMap> lineitem_date_zones. During load, compute zone every 8192 rows. In queries/q6.cpp, iterate zones: if (zone.max_date < start_date || zone.min_date >= end_date) skip; else scan rows [zone.start_row, zone.end_row). Expected improvement: 150-200ms (skip 86% of data). Q6 drops from ~150ms to ~50ms.
recommendations.expected_improvement: 150-200ms reduction (skip 86% of blocks). Combined with SIMD (priority 4), Q6 drops from 257ms → 50-70ms (close to DuckDB's 27ms).

recommendations.technique: Pre-size hash tables to avoid rehashing
recommendations.description: Current code uses std::unordered_set and std::unordered_map with default initial sizes, causing multiple rehashes during build phase. Rehashing is expensive: copy all entries, recompute buckets, ~3x overhead. Pre-size based on cardinality estimates: (1) qualifying_customers: expect 20% of 1.5M = 300K customers, (2) qualifying_orders: expect 46% of orders from 300K customers = ~690K orders, (3) aggregates: expect ~300K groups. Implementation: In queries/q3.cpp, add: qualifying_customers.reserve(400000); qualifying_orders.reserve(1000000); aggregates.reserve(500000). Use load factor 0.75 so reserves create appropriately-sized buckets. Expected improvement: 50-100ms (avoid 2-3 rehash operations).
recommendations.expected_improvement: 50-100ms reduction (2-5% of Q3 time). Small but free optimization (one line per hash table).

recommendations.description: After implementing perfect hashing (priority 3), further optimize by processing lineitem in 2048-row batches and using SIMD for arithmetic. Current code: for each row, compute disc_price and charge, update 6 aggregates. Vectorized: load 4 doubles (l_extendedprice[i:i+4]) into __m256d, load 4 discounts, compute disc_price = price * (1.0 - discount) using _mm256_mul_pd, accumulate into vector registers, periodically flush to scalar aggregates. Implementation: In queries/q1.cpp, after perfect hashing, add AVX2 batch processing. Process rows in groups of 4 (AVX2 handles 4 doubles). Use __m256d accumulators for sum_qty, sum_base_price, etc. Expected improvement: 100-200ms (2-3x speedup on arithmetic, but still bottlenecked by memory bandwidth).
recommendations.expected_improvement: 100-200ms reduction after perfect hashing. Brings Q1 from ~700ms to ~500-600ms. Diminishing returns due to memory bandwidth limits.

recommendations.technique: Replace atoi/atof with fast_float parsing library
recommendations.description: Data loading takes 141s for 76.5M total rows (lineitem 60M + orders 15M + customer 1.5M). That's ~540K rows/sec, extremely slow. Current parse_line uses std::atoi and std::atof which are slow (locale-aware, error handling). fast_float library (header-only) provides 4-10x faster float parsing. For integers, use custom parsing: int parse_int(const char* s) { int v=0; while(*s) v=v*10+(*s++-'0'); return v; }. Implementation: In storage/storage.cpp, replace std::atof(fields[i].c_str()) with fast_float::from_chars. Replace std::atoi with custom parse_int. Expected improvement: 70-100s reduction in load time (from 141s to ~40-70s). Does not affect query execution.
recommendations.expected_improvement: 70-100s reduction in data load time (50-70% faster loading). No impact on query execution time. Trade-off: better for iterative development (faster iteration cycles).
recommendations.knowledge_source: external-libs (fast_float is widely used) + novel application

recommendations.technique: Fused join-aggregation loop to reduce hash table lookups
recommendations.description: Current Q3 does two hash table probes per qualifying lineitem row: (1) probe qualifying_orders to get (o_orderdate, o_shippriority), (2) probe aggregates hash table with composite key. Instead, build hash table that maps l_orderkey directly to aggregate pointer. During customer-orders join, create map<int32_t, Q3Aggregate*> orderkey_to_agg. When scanning lineitem, single probe retrieves aggregate pointer, update in-place. This halves hash table probes (from 2 to 1 per row). Implementation: In queries/q3.cpp, after customer-orders join, create unordered_map<int32_t, Q3Aggregate*> orderkey_to_agg_ptr. Allocate aggregates upfront (vector<Q3Aggregate> agg_storage), store pointers in map. In lineitem loop: auto* agg = orderkey_to_agg_ptr[lineitem.l_orderkey[i]]; if (agg) agg->revenue += computed_revenue. Expected improvement: 100-200ms (eliminate half of hash probes).
recommendations.expected_improvement: 100-200ms reduction (5-10% of Q3 time). Reduces hash table overhead. Combined with bloom filter, brings Q3 to ~600-800ms.

storage_changes.change: Sort by l_shipdate during data load
storage_changes.rationale: All 3 queries filter on l_shipdate. Sorting enables: (1) zone maps with tight min/max per block (skip 86% of blocks in Q6), (2) binary search for range boundaries, (3) sequential access after filter improves prefetching. Cost: 10-20s added to load time. Benefit: 5-10x speedup on Q6, enables zone maps for Q1/Q3.

storage_changes.change: Build zone maps on l_shipdate (8K row granularity)
storage_changes.rationale: After sorting, zone maps provide coarse-grained pruning. For Q6 (date range [1994-01-01, 1995-01-01)), skip blocks where zone.max_date < 1994-01-01 or zone.min_date >= 1995-01-01. Expected: skip 86% of 7500 blocks (60M rows / 8K). Memory overhead: 7500 zones × 16 bytes = 120KB (negligible). Speed up Q6 by 5-8x.

summary: Iteration 1 baseline is 6-16x slower than DuckDB. Top 3 optimizations by expected impact: (1) Q3 bloom filter (800-1200ms saved, 37-55% speedup), (2) Q1 perfect hashing (400-600ms saved, 33-50% speedup), (3) Q6 zone maps (150-200ms saved after sorting, 58-78% speedup). Combined impact: Q1 from 1210ms→500-600ms, Q3 from 2178ms→600-800ms, Q6 from 257ms→50-70ms. Total query time from 3645ms→1150-1470ms (60-68% reduction). Critical path: implement Q3 bloom filter + top-K heap (priorities 1-2), Q1 perfect hashing (priority 3), then zone maps (priorities 5-6). Lower priorities (7-10) provide incremental 5-20% gains. Expected final performance after all optimizations: Q1 ~400-500ms (2-2.5x DuckDB), Q3 ~400-600ms (3-4.5x DuckDB), Q6 ~30-50ms (1.1-1.8x DuckDB). Remaining gap suggests need for more advanced techniques in iteration 2: compiled queries, parallel execution, or deeper storage optimizations.
